pages_preview.text_preview: Training Protocol for Monitors NRG Metaketa III August 28, 2017 Preliminaries This document describes the principles for training monitors as part of the Metaketa III Natural Resource Governance project. It is based on the premise that field conditions will make it impossible to fully harmonize how monitors are trained, but that each team should comply with common principles and procedures for training. 1 Verification First, the trainers will verify that the selected community members meet the appropriate criteria for being selected as monitors. The general criteria for the Metaketa as a whole are enumerated in a separate document. Each team will produce a written list of specific criteria it uses for the selection of monitors. If the criteria are met, the monitors will be asked to give their consent and training will commence. If the criteria are not met or consent is not given for any or all individuals, replacement monitors will be selected following the selection protocol. 2 Background All monitors should receive a presentation that lays out what community monitoring is, why they are being asked to monitor, about the resource that they are being asked to monitor, how that resource responds to human use, and some of the consequences of resource use and/or degradation. Potential monitors should receive enough background information that they are able to explain the purpose of the project to both users of the resource and third- parties. 3 Responsibilities Everymonitorshouldreceiveinformationemphasizingtheresponsibilitythattheyaretaking on in choosing to be a monitor and how their actions as a monitor could contribute to the 1

collective good. This part of the training should be motivational in line with the cultural contextofthestudy. Thegoalofemphasizingtrainingistoencourageasenseofresponsibility for compliance with the monitoring protocol. 4 Conceptual Training The actual responsibilities of monitors should be laid out verbally and all monitors should be provided with a appropriate reference material that lays out all of the steps that they will take to carry out the protocol. The conceptual training should include the following elements: • Information about the measures employed, their scales, and how they relate to human welfare; • Information delineating the responsibilities among teams of monitors, including lead- ership roles and/or coordination of monitoring; • Timing and frequency of monitoring; • Medium to record the observed data (e.g. a field journal); • Method of information dissemination. In addition, all monitors should be trained on how they would respond to questions about their actions from relevant groups, such as users, government officials, businesses, etc. To facilitate their work, all monitors should have access to some kind of letter of introduc- tion or official document recognizing their role as a monitor by either the research team or implementing partner (e.g., letter of introduction, project ID card, project information card). 5 Technical Training All monitors should receive a presentation or demonstration of the actual monitoring task that they will be asked to perform as part of their monitoring duties. As part of the training and in the presence of trainers, they should have the ability to perform the full monitoring protocol, including record-keeping and transmission of records as necessary, and to ask any questions of trainers. Before monitors work independently, they will receive feedback from trainers, who will either accompany or debrief monitors for their first field run. 6 Information Dissemination Training All monitors will receive training about how to record observations in a medium that will be durable and eventually accessible to all of the research teams and third parties, to ensure fidelity of the monitoring data. In initial training meetings, this will take the form of a demonstration to accompany the technical training. Each monitor will have the opportunity foratestrunofsubmissionoftheinformation, withquestionsansweredandfeedbackoffered. 2

7 Feedback and Discussion All trainees will have an opportunity to raise questions, discuss any points of confusion, and discuss possible modifications with the trainers. The trainers should take note of common difficulties and report back to the coordinating teams, as appropriate, to ensure consistent training implementation. 8 Testing and Comprehension Prior to launching monitoring, the work of all monitors who take part in training and test runsshallbeassessedforquality. Allteamswilldocumentthestandardsemployedforquality checks as part of their project-specific protocols to be implemented prior to “graduating” monitors into the actual field experiments. 3
